perturbation.original_text: Boxlot shall deliver to theglobe all information about Users who register with Boxlot on the Co-Branded Pages that Boxlot collects from its registration process. Such information shall be delivered weekly in an electronic format specified by theglobe using a process reasonably specified by theglobe.
perturbation.changed_text: Boxlot may deliver to theglobe all information about Users who register with Boxlot on the Co-Branded Pages that Boxlot collects from its registration process. Such information shall be delivered bi-weekly in a physical format chosen by Boxlot.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'Boxlot shall deliver' which is an obligation. The changed text says 'Boxlot may deliver,' making it optional, introducing uncertainty. Additionally, the original delivery is 'weekly in an electronic format' but after perturbation it turns into 'bi-weekly in a physical format' creating an in-text contradiction on the delivery method, making it more confusing when to deliver, the format, etc.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2 Delivery to theglobe. Boxlot may deliver to theglobe all information about Users who register with Boxlot on the Co-Branded Pages that Boxlot collects from its registration process. Such information shall be delivered bi-weekly in a physical format chosen by Boxlot.
perturbation.justification: The changed text makes the delivery of user information optional ('may deliver') and changes the delivery schedule and format (bi-weekly, physical) which directly contradicts the same statement repeated in the contradicted text. This creates an in-text contradiction because the altered clause impacts obligations established elsewhere in the agreement and renders them uncertain. It falls under the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction since the contract is now using conflicting definitions (shall vs may, weekly electronic vs bi-weekly physical).

perturbation.original_text: The Service generally shall be publicly available to Users a minimum of 95% of the time during any 7 day period and 98% of the time during any 30 day period, excluding "Maintenance." Maintenance is defined as scheduled Service outages for Service maintenance or upgrades of which theglobe is notified at least 48 hours in advance, so long as such outages are scheduled for low-usage time periods and do not exceed a total of 20 hours in any 30 day period.
perturbation.changed_text: The Platform generally should be publicly accessible to Clients a minimum of 85% of the time during any 7 day period and 78% of the time during any 30 day period, excluding "Downtime." Downtime is defined as unscheduled Platform outages for Platform maintenance or upgrades of which theglobe is notified at least 24 hours in advance, so long as such outages are scheduled for high-usage time periods and should exceed a total of 40 hours in any 30 day period.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces multiple contradictions. 'Service' is replaced with 'Platform,' and 'Users' with 'Clients,' without clarifying if these are the same. Availability percentages are significantly reduced (95% to 85% and 98% to 78%). 'Maintenance' is replaced with 'Downtime', and the advance notification period is changed from 48 to 24 hours. Finally, scheduled for 'low-usage' turns into 'high-usage', and the total time is modified to 'should exceed a total of 40 hours', creating confusion about whether the goal is minimize it or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: EXHIBIT B PERFORMANCE STANDARDS A. Service Availability. The Platform generally should be publicly accessible to Clients a minimum of 85% of the time during any 7 day period and 78% of the time during any 30 day period, excluding "Downtime." Downtime is defined as unscheduled Platform outages for Platform maintenance or upgrades of which theglobe is notified at least 24 hours in advance, so long as such outages are scheduled for high-usage time periods and should exceed a total of 40 hours in any 30 day period.
perturbation.justification: The Changed Text and Contradicted Text are identical, suggesting the change was intended to align with the existing language. While the explanation details potential inconsistencies created by the changes from the original text, the fact that the 'Contradicted Text' is identical to the 'Changed Text' indicates there is no in-text contradiction present, given the definition of in-text contradiction requires two different parts of the document to be in conflict.

perturbation.original_text: theglobe may request that Boxlot Banners be co-branded with theglobe Marks, in which case the parties shall work together to develop a mutually acceptable implementation. theglobe may approve or reject any Boxlot Banner in its sole discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: theglobe can mandate that Boxlot Commercials be co-branded with theglobe Brands, in which case the parties shall ignore working together to develop a mutually acceptable implementation. theglobe must approve or reject any Boxlot Commercial in its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'request' to 'mandate' alters the power dynamic. 'Banners' becomes 'Commercials,' and 'Marks' becomes 'Brands,' without clarifying the relationship between these terms. Removing the collaborative approach ('shall ignore working together') directly conflicts with the original intent. 'theglobe may approve or reject' turns into 'theglobe must approve or reject' making it an obligation. Making contradicting meaning by doing this perturbation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.3 Boxlot Banners. Boxlot shall deliver to theglobe any Boxlot Banners which are to be run in accordance with this Agreement. Such banners shall comply with theglobe's then-current technical standards. The terms of any insertion order or similar document regarding the Boxlot Banners are expressly rejected, except to the extent that they specify the location, timing or duration of the display of the Boxlot Banners and such terms are accepted by theglobe. Unless mutually agreed otherwise, Boxlot Banners shall link to the Co-Branded Pages. theglobe can mandate that Boxlot Commercials be co-branded with theglobe Brands, in which case the parties shall ignore working together to develop a mutually acceptable implementation. theglobe must approve or reject any Boxlot Commercial in its sole discretion. theglobe shall use commercially reasonable efforts not to run banners on the Co-Branded Pages for the companies enumerated on Exhibit C. Boxlot may update Exhibit C from time to time during the term; however: (a) Boxlot may not add any site or company to Exhibit C which is not reasonably considered to be in the online auctions business, and (b) if Boxlot adds any new sites or companies to Exhibit C, theglobe shall be entitled to complete any contractual obligations pursuant to contracts in place with such sites or companies at the time of addition.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the nature of co-branding from a 'request' to a 'mandate' and changes 'shall work together' to 'shall ignore working together', which directly contradicts the collaborative nature implied elsewhere in the contradicted text regarding Boxlot Banners. The change from 'Banners' to 'Commercials' without clearly defining the relationship also creates ambiguity, which falls under misaligned terminology. Additionally, changing 'may approve or reject' to 'must approve or reject' introduces conflicting obligations with other sections where 'may' is used, increasing the chances of obligations breaking. This results in a strong in-text contradiction based on the provided definition.
